Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.

- Plan: Collect all 7 green blocks, then do ACTION7. Total: 8 actions. This is a clean test of the hypothesis that collecting blocks + ACTION7 completes the level.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model.

When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 372 actions; a strong score needs about 21 or fewer. You are 17.7x over the target.
NET-ZERO WASTE: your last 65 actions returned the board to a state already seen 65 actions ago — that round-trip made no progress yet still cost 65 actions. Do not extend-and-retract or oscillate; a sequence that ends where it began is pure waste.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! After collecting 7 green blocks and doing ACTION7, there's 1 green block remaining. Looking at the image, I see 1 green block in the top-right area. This is different from before - ACTION7 only spawned 1 block instead of 7 or 9.
Wait, let me look more carefully. The image shows:
- Top area: no green blocks (they were collected)
- Middle area: no green blocks (they were collected)
- Top-right: 1 green block
So ACTION7 spawned only 1 green block. This is different from the earlier runs where ACTION7 spawned 7 or 9 blocks.
Why did ACTION7 only spawn 1 block this time? Maybe because I collected the blocks in a specific order (left to right), or maybe because the player was at a specific position.
Let me collect this 1 block and see what happens. Maybe the level completes when all blocks are collected.
Actually, wait. Looking at the image more carefully, I see the green block is in the top-right area. Let me check its position.
The green block appears to be in the top sky blue area, near the right edge. Let me collect it and see if the level completes.
But first, let me check: did ACTION7 spawn 1 block or did 1 block remain from the original 7? Looking at the image, the block is in a different position than the original 7 blocks. The original blocks were at cols 15, 21, 27 (top) and 33, 39, 45, 51 (middle). The remaining block is in the top-right, which is a different position.
So ACTION7 spawned 1 new block. This is interesting. Maybe the number of blocks spawned by ACTION7 depends on how many blocks were collected or in what order.
Let me collect this 1 block and see if the level completes.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content: After collecting 7 blocks + ACTION7, only 1 green block spawned (not 7 or 9 as before). This is different! Let me collect it and check if level completes.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
seg = current_frame.segmentation

# Find the remaining green block
greens = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'N']
print(f"Green blocks: {len(greens)}")

for g in greens:
    boundary = g['boundary']
    rows = [p[0] for p in boundary]
    cols = [p[1] for p in boundary]
    center_row = (min(rows) + max(rows)) // 2
    center_col = (min(cols) + max(cols)) // 2
    print(f"Green: rows {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, cols {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}, center=({center_row}, {center_col})")
    
    # Click on it
    action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': center_row, 'col': center_col}])
    
    print(f"Level completed: {last_action_result.get('level_completed', False)}")
    print(f"Game over: {last_action_result.get('game_over', False)}")
    
    # Check state after click
    seg = current_frame.segmentation
    greens = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'N']
    print(f"Green blocks after click: {len(greens)}")
    
    # Check all non-background objects
    for n in seg['nodes']:
        if n['color'] not in ('B', 'S', 'p', 'W', 'G'):
            boundary = n['boundary']
            rows = [p[0] for p in boundary]
            cols = [p[1] for p in boundary]
            print(f"  {n['color']}: {n['pixels']} px, rows {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, cols {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
